eli3: Standard deviation is the square root of variance. It answers: on average, how far do individual data points sit from the mean? Taking the square root brings the unit back to the original scale ‚ÄĒ so if data is in dollars, standard deviation is also in dollars.

For a normal distribution, about 68% of data falls within ¬Ī1ŌÉ of the mean, 95% within ¬Ī2ŌÉ, and 99.7% within ¬Ī3ŌÉ. This '68-95-99.7 rule' is one of the most-used rules of thumb in statistics and ML.

Standard deviation is the natural measure of spread for normal-like data. It also appears in the formula for z-scores: z = (x‚ąíőľ)/ŌÉ, which tells you how many standard deviations a value is from the mean ‚ÄĒ the basis for standardisation, z-tests, and normal tables.

whenWhy.title: Measuring and comparing spread across variables
whenWhy.body: Standard deviation expresses spread in the same units as the data (unlike variance, which is in squared units). It enables direct comparison: 'these test scores have ŌÉ=10, those have ŌÉ=5' ‚ÄĒ the first group is twice as spread out.

whenWhy.title: Standardisation / z-score normalisation
whenWhy.body: z = (x‚ąíőľ)/ŌÉ transforms data to mean 0, std 1. This is critical before algorithms that are sensitive to feature scale (KNN, SVM, PCA, neural networks). Without standardisation, features with large scales dominate distance-based and gradient-based computations.

whenWhy.title: Confidence intervals and hypothesis tests
whenWhy.body: The standard error of the mean (ŌÉ/‚ąön) is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of xŐĄ. It quantifies how precisely the sample mean estimates the population mean. A 95% CI for őľ is approximately xŐĄ ¬Ī 1.96ŌÉ/‚ąön. All z-tests and t-tests normalise by the standard error.

whenWhy.title: Volatility in finance
whenWhy.body: Annualised volatility of a financial asset is the annualised standard deviation of log-returns: ŌÉ_annual = ŌÉ_daily √ó ‚ąö252. It is the core input to Black-Scholes option pricing and risk measures like VaR. Higher ŌÉ means more uncertain outcomes.

deepDiveIntro: Sample standard deviation s = ‚ąös¬≤ = ‚ąö[ő£(xŠĶĘ‚ąíxŐĄ)¬≤/(n‚ąí1)]. Note: s is NOT an unbiased estimator of ŌÉ even though s¬≤ is unbiased for ŌÉ¬≤. This is because E[‚ąös¬≤] ‚Č† ‚ąöE[s¬≤] by Jensen's inequality (‚ąö is concave). The bias is small for large n and is rarely corrected in practice.

For a normal distribution N(őľ,ŌÉ¬≤), the standard deviation ŌÉ fully characterises the spread. The empirical rule (68-95-99.7) follows directly from the normal CDF: ő¶(1)‚ąíő¶(‚ąí1) ‚Čą 0.683, ő¶(2)‚ąíő¶(‚ąí2) ‚Čą 0.954, ő¶(3)‚ąíő¶(‚ąí3) ‚Čą 0.997.

deepDive.heading: Definition and Relationship to Variance
deepDive.body: ŌÉ = ‚ąöŌÉ¬≤ (population); s = ‚ąös¬≤ = ‚ąö[ő£(xŠĶĘ‚ąíxŐĄ)¬≤/(n‚ąí1)] (sample). Units: same as x. s¬≤ is unbiased for ŌÉ¬≤, but s is not unbiased for ŌÉ due to Jensen's inequality. For normal populations, the correction factor is c‚āĄ(n) = ‚ąö(2/(n‚ąí1)) ¬∑ őď(n/2)/őď((n‚ąí1)/2); for large n, c‚āĄ ‚Čą 1 ‚ąí 1/(4(n‚ąí1)). [1]

deepDive.heading: Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7)
deepDive.body: For X ~ N(őľ,ŌÉ¬≤): P(|X‚ąíőľ| ‚Č§ ŌÉ) ‚Čą 0.6827; P(|X‚ąíőľ| ‚Č§ 2ŌÉ) ‚Čą 0.9545; P(|X‚ąíőľ| ‚Č§ 3ŌÉ) ‚Čą 0.9973. These follow from the standard normal CDF. For non-normal distributions, Chebyshev's inequality gives a weaker but universal bound: P(|X‚ąíőľ| ‚Č• kŌÉ) ‚Č§ 1/k¬≤ for any distribution with finite variance. [1]

deepDive.heading: Standard Error of the Mean
deepDive.body: SE(xŐĄ) = ŌÉ/‚ąön (known ŌÉ) or s/‚ąön (estimated). This is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of xŐĄ. It shrinks as 1/‚ąön ‚ÄĒ doubling precision requires 4√ó more data. The 95% CI for őľ is xŐĄ ¬Ī z_{0.025} ¬∑ ŌÉ/‚ąön = xŐĄ ¬Ī 1.96 ¬∑ ŌÉ/‚ąön for large n, or xŐĄ ¬Ī t_{n‚ąí1,0.025} ¬∑ s/‚ąön for small n. [1]

deepDive.heading: Z-score Standardisation
deepDive.body: z = (x‚ąíőľ)/ŌÉ. Under N(őľ,ŌÉ¬≤), z ~ N(0,1). In ML, feature standardisation (sklearn's StandardScaler) applies z = (x‚ąíxŐĄ)/s. This is essential for: (1) PCA ‚ÄĒ so components reflect variance structure, not scale; (2) gradient descent ‚ÄĒ so learning rates are well-conditioned; (3) regularisation ‚ÄĒ so L1/L2 penalties are applied equally across features. [1][2]

takeaways: s = ‚ąö[ő£(xŠĶĘ‚ąíxŐĄ)¬≤/(n‚ąí1)]; same units as x ‚ÄĒ more interpretable than variance. [1]
s¬≤ is unbiased for ŌÉ¬≤, but s is slightly biased for ŌÉ (Jensen's inequality ‚ÄĒ minor for n>30). [1]
68-95-99.7 rule: for N(őľ,ŌÉ¬≤), ‚Čą68/95/99.7% of data falls within 1/2/3ŌÉ of mean. [1]
Standard error SE = ŌÉ/‚ąön: standard deviation of the sampling distribution of xŐĄ. [1]
Z-score z=(x‚ąíőľ)/ŌÉ standardises to N(0,1) ‚ÄĒ basis of z-tests and feature scaling. [1][2]
Chebyshev: P(|X‚ąíőľ| ‚Č• kŌÉ) ‚Č§ 1/k¬≤ ‚ÄĒ distribution-free bound using only ŌÉ. [1]

edgeCases.q: Why is the coefficient of variation (CV) sometimes preferred over standard deviation?
edgeCases.a: CV = ŌÉ/őľ √ó 100% is a dimensionless measure of relative variability. It allows comparing spread across variables with different scales or units: a ŌÉ=5 may be large if őľ=10 (CV=50%) but small if őľ=1000 (CV=0.5%). CV is meaningful only for ratio-scale data with a positive mean (not for data that can be zero or negative). [1]

edgeCases.q: When should you use MAD instead of standard deviation?
edgeCases.a: MAD = median(|xŠĶĘ‚ąímedian(x)|) is a robust alternative to ŌÉ. It has breakdown point 0.5 (like the median) vs ŌÉ's breakdown point of 0. Robust ŌÉŐā = 1.4826 √ó MAD is a consistent estimator of ŌÉ for normal data. Use MAD when data has heavy tails, or when robustness to outliers is more important than efficiency under normality. [2]

sources.label: Casella & Berger ‚ÄĒ Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 2
